coredata.dc:title: Innovative graphene quantum dots applications for enhancing lutein biosynthesis in microalgae biorefineries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Patel, Anil Kumar
Sumathi, Yamini
Singhania, Reeta Rani
Chen, Chiu-Wen
Michaud, Philippe
Dong, Cheng-Di
coredata.dc:description: Lutein, a potent antioxidant xanthophyll used in ocular and health applications, is produced by microalgae during growth rather than stress stages. Therefore, its production faces challenges due to competition with growth-related metabolites. This study presents a novel approach using quantum dots to enhance lutein yield by exposing microalgal cultures to Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) concentrations (0–200 mg/L) within a pre-optimized lutein bioprocess. Additionally, nitrogen source selection (NH4Cl or urea) is crucial in balancing growth and lutein biosynthesis. Biomass production peaked at 100 mg/L GODs, yielding 7.46 g/L in NH4Cl and 6.58 g/L in urea-supplemented media. Lutein content was notably higher in the urea medium, reaching 17.95 mg/g with a total yield of 118.2 mg/L, likely due to slower nitrogen uptake, which favored lutein accumulation compared to NH4Cl with inclined growth support. Bioenergy potential ranged from 895-985 kJ in the lutein-omitted biomass, enhanced by GQDs based on their composition. Graphene QDs addition enhanced total lutein yield by approx. 30.5 % over quantum dot-free processes. This integrated biorefinery approach demonstrates a sustainable strategy to boost bioactive compound production for pharmaceutical and biofuel applications through controlled quantum dot use in microalgae cultivation. It is aligning with Sustainable Development Goals for resource efficiency, climate action (SDG 13) and sustainable industrial practices (SDG 9).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum Dots
Nanomaterials
Lutein
Microalgae
Chlorella variabilis
Sustainable Development Goals 9

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microalgae culture and graphene quantum dots treatment
Effect of graphene quantum dots on biomass yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on lutein yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on protein yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on carbohydrate yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on chlorophyll yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on lipid yield
Effect of graphene quantum dots on overall bioenergy potential
Effect of graphene quantum dots in oxidative stress

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of concentrations of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the microalgal biomass enhancement under varying nitrogen sources: (a) NH4Cl; (b) Urea [Growth conditions: 10 k lux light intensity, 14 days cultivation, 3x nutrients]. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.

Effect of GQDs concentrations on microalgal lutein enhancement under varying nitrogen sources: (a) NH4Cl; (b) Urea. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.

Effect of GQDs concentrations in microalgal metabolites accumulation: (a) Proteins; (b) Carbohydrates; (c) Lipids. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.

Effect of GQDs concentrations on the microalgal chlorophyll content under varying nitrogen sources: (a) Chlorophyll a with NH4Cl; (b) Chlorophyll b with NH4Cl; (c) Chlorophyll a with urea (d) Chlorophyll b with urea. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.
Heatmaps illustrate the effects of GQDs concentrations on lipid, carbohydrate and protein content under various nitrogen sources: (a) NH4Cl; (b) Urea.

Effect of GQDs concentrations on the bioenergy potential (in KJ) of lutein free biomass of Chlorella variabilis. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.

Effect of GQDs concentrations on microalgal stress-induced enzymatic assays: (a) Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity; (b) Catalase (CAT) activity. Different English letters over the bars indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) by one-way ANOVA.

head.ce:title: Innovative graphene quantum dots applications for enhancing lutein biosynthesis in microalgae biorefineries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Clermont Auvergne, Clermont Auvergne INP, CNRS, Institute Pascal, 63000 Clermont-Ferrand, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Université Clermont Auvergne, Clermont Auvergne INP, CNRS, Institute Pascal, 63000 Clermont-Ferrand, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrated lutein-lipid bioprocess improvement using innovative Quantum Dots.

Lutein, a potent antioxidant xanthophyll used in ocular and health applications, is produced by microalgae during growth rather than stress stages. Therefore, its production faces challenges due to competition with growth-related metabolites. This study presents a novel approach using quantum dots to enhance lutein yield by exposing microalgal cultures to Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) concentrations (0–200 mg/L) within a pre-optimized lutein bioprocess. Additionally, nitrogen source selection (NH4Cl or urea) is crucial in balancing growth and lutein biosynthesis. Biomass production peaked at 100 mg/L GODs, yielding 7.46 g/L in NH4Cl and 6.58 g/L in urea-supplemented media. Lutein content was notably higher in the urea medium, reaching 17.95 mg/g with a total yield of 118.2 mg/L, likely due to slower nitrogen uptake, which favored lutein accumulation compared to NH4Cl with inclined growth support. Bioenergy potential ranged from 895-985 kJ in the lutein-omitted biomass, enhanced by GQDs based on their composition. Graphene QDs addition enhanced total lutein yield by approx. 30.5 % over quantum dot-free processes. This integrated biorefinery approach demonstrates a sustainable strategy to boost bioactive compound production for pharmaceutical and biofuel applications through controlled quantum dot use in microalgae cultivation. It is aligning with Sustainable Development Goals for resource efficiency, climate action (SDG 13) and sustainable industrial practices (SDG 9).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae have become an important platform for sustainable bioeconomy applications due to their capacity to sequester CO2, produce bioactive compounds, and serve as precursors for bioactive compounds and biofuels [1,2]. In addition to their role in carbon mitigation fixing approx. 1.83 tons of CO2 per ton of dry biomass [3], microalgae efficiently convert solar energy into valuable compounds such as carotenoids, polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs), and lipids, all with applications in food, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and biofuels [4]. Among these, lutein stands out for its antioxidant properties, making it a highly preferred ingredient for ocular health supplements and skincare products with a growing market demand of USD 357.7 million by 2024 [1,5].

The integrated biorefinery concept has gained momentum as a sustainable approach to maximize resource recovery from microalgal biomass, allowing for the simultaneous production of biofuels and high-value compounds like lutein, and astaxanthin [6,7]. This approach is designed to valorize the full spectrum of microalgal metabolites such as lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates, while minimizing waste and improving economic feasibility [8]. Unlike traditional single-product processes, integrated biorefineries strategically combine multiple production and extraction processes to yield biofuels alongside bioactive compounds. This multi-product approach not only reduces environmental impact by making full use of biomass but also aligns with zero-waste goals, as residual biomass from one stage can serve as feedstock for subsequent stages [8,9]. By integrating lutein production with biofuels, biorefineries meet rising market demands for bioactive ingredients in pharmaceuticals and cosmetics while supporting sustainable energy goals [10]. This synergy promotes a circular bioeconomy, where the renewable production of biofuels and nutraceuticals reduces reliance on fossil resources and lowers greenhouse gas emissions [3]. As such, integrated biorefineries reflect a shift toward environmentally conscious production practices, addressing both resource efficiency and the pressing need for sustainable industrial solutions [11,12].

Nanotechnology advancements have led to nanoparticles (NPs) displaying unique properties, such as surface and quantum size effects, enhancing applications across fields like biomedicine, environmental science, bioprocessing, and energy production [13,14]. In microalgae cultivation, nanotechnology has emerged as a powerful tool to boost biomass yield and metabolite production. Studies indicate that NPs, at optimal concentrations, can enhance cellular growth and target compound synthesis [15]. For example, nanomaterials can improve photosynthetic efficiency by up to 40 % by optimizing light use and triggering oxidative stress pathways that increase valuable metabolite accumulation [16].

Quantum-size nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs), offer further benefits with unique optical and electronic properties. These semiconducting materials can alter excitation and emission wavelengths by adjusting size and shape, enhancing solar light conversion and microalgal biomass production [17,18]. Carbon dots (CDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are particularly promising due to their biocompatibility, solubility, low toxicity, and eco-friendliness [19]. GOQDs, for example, improve dispersibility in water, aiding bio- and medical applications [20]. Graphene and its derivatives, such as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), also show significant potential due to broad-spectrum light absorption, high surface area, and electrical conductivity. Studies reveal that graphene can enhance microalgal carbon fixation by up to 20 % and lipid accumulation by 30 %, making it valuable for sustainable biofuel applications [21,22].

While nanomaterials have demonstrated potential to enhance biomass and carotenoid yields in microalgal bioprocesses, their effects within optimized systems remain largely unexplored, often resulting in limited insights due to weak metabolic flux enhancements. This study uniquely addresses the lutein production challenges by integrating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into a pre-optimized bioprocess, aiming to boost both biomass yield and lutein synthesis sustainably. Notably, this is the first report demonstrating significant biomass alongside lutein yields exceeding 100 mg/L scale, achieved through a uniquely designed bioprocess with quantum dot integration. This graphene-based approach advances sustainable bioprocessing, enhancing productivity while supporting a circular green economy by promoting carbon sequestration, reducing energy inputs, and minimizing waste. The optimized system also shows promise for biofuel production, with the potential to significantly reduce CO2 emissions while generating high-value bioactive compounds for the food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries. This graphene-enabled bioprocess aligns with Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 12 and 13, supporting resource-efficient, climate-resilient solutions that advance clean energy and sustainable industry practices.

All the chemicals used for the production and analysis were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Analytical grade reagents were used, and extraction solvents such as methanol, ethanol, dichloromethane, acetonitrile, diethyl ether, and acetone were obtained from J.T Baker and Honeywell. Graphene quantum dots were commercially purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific (USA), ranging in size from 2-10 nm. The unique manufacturing processes used are non-oxidizing, resulting in a pristine graphitic surface comprised of sp2 carbon molecules. This makes the material particularly suitable for applications demanding high electrical or thermal conductivity. It is characterized by its high surface area and significant macro-porosity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microalgae culture and graphene quantum dots treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The microalgae Chlorella variabilis was grown in Tris Acetate Phosphate (TAP) media and is suitable for growing under mixotrophic cultivation mode to produce biomass and lutein. The media composition (per liter) for Bejernick solution (BS; macronutrient stock)- 7.5 mM NH4Cl, 0.4 mM MgSO4, 0.35 mM CaCl2; Phosphate buffer: 0.69 mM Na2HPO4, 0.45 mM KH2PO4; Tris-acetate buffer: 20 mM trisma base, 17 mM acetic acid; and 1 mL of Hutner's trace (HT, micronutrients stock) were added to the 1L tubular photobioreactors with an working volume of 700 mL for 14 days with proper light intensity of 10 k lux and carbon dioxide purged at 5 % with 25 cc/min flow rate and temperature kept 28˚ C.

In another set, except for the nitrogen source 7.5 mM NH4Cl, Urea is replaced as a modification of the nitrogen source in place of NH4Cl. Antifoam B emulsion was added to prevent the foam formation. Additionally, 20 mM KOH was added to maintain the pH of the microalgae at 7.5 in a bicarbonate buffer system and supply inorganic carbon for photosynthesis. Different concentrations of graphene quantum dots (50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L) were dispersed into the medium and autoclaved. After autoclaving, the medium was purged to ensure uniform graphene dispersion, followed by inoculation with microalgae at a concentration not exceeding 0.1.

For dry biomass estimation, 30 mL of microalgae samples were collected at 2-day intervals from day 4 to day 14. On the final day, the remaining culture was harvested. The collected samples were centrifuged, and the biomass was stored at −20 °C until further processing. The frozen biomass was then freeze-dried to remove all moisture content. The freeze-dried biomass was weighed, and the final biomass yield was calculated to determine the growth and productivity of the microalgae over the cultivation period.

In a grinding tube, 10 mg of algal dried biomass was mixed with 0.15 g of zirconium beads and ground for a total of 3 min with 2 min of dry beating and 1 min of solvent beating. The solvent mixture was of methanol and dichloromethane (3:2). After 3 min of bead beating, a 0.22-µm membrane filter was used to filter the extraction mixture, which was then used for HPLC analysis, as defined in the following section.

The optical density of microalgal cells at 750 nm (OD 750) was determined using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The light intensity (lux) was measured with a photometer (LUTRON LX-1108, Czech Republic), while the pH of the culture medium was determined using a digital pH meter (Denver Instrument UltraBasic EW-59503–00 model). Lutein quantification was carried out using an HPLC system featuring an isocratic pump, autosampler, and PDA detector, managed by Finnigan ChromQuest Chromatography software (version 4.2). The separation was achieved on a C-18 reverse-phase column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 µm particle size, Kanto Chem. Co. Inc., Japan). The mobile phase, flowing at 1 mL/min at 35 °C, consisted of solvent A (methanol/dichloromethane/acetonitrile/water, 85:5:5.5:4.5 v/v) and solvent B (methanol/dichloromethane/acetonitrile/water, 28:25:42.5:4.5 v/v), with detection at 450 nm. This method was adapted from Patel et al. [7], and lutein standards were used to quantify the lutein concentration in the liquid samples obtained from dry biomass of the microalgae.

The protein content was determined using Lowry method. To determine the protein content, a series of reagents were prepared as follows: Solution A, consisting of 2 % Na2CO3 (anhydrous) in 0.1 N NaOH, was made by dissolving 2 g of Na2CO3 and 400 mg of NaOH in 100 mL of distilled water. Solution B, comprising 0.5 % CuSO4·5H2O in 1 % sodium potassium tartrate, was prepared by dissolving 50 mg of CuSO4·5H2O and 100 mg of sodium potassium tartrate in 10 mL of distilled water. Solution C was obtained by mixing Solutions A and B in a 5:1 ratio. Additionally, Solution D was prepared as a 1 % (v/v) Folin-Ciocalteu reagent (2 N). For the assay, 5 mg of freeze-dried microalgae was placed in a grinding tube with 150 mg of zirconium beads, followed by the addition of 2 mL of 0.5 M NaOH to disrupt the cells for 1.5 min. The homogenates were then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 2 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of the supernatant was transferred to a test tube, and 5 mL of Solution C was added, followed by incubation at room temperature for 10 min. After incubation, 500 µL of Solution D was added, and the mixture was incubated in the dark for 30 min. The absorbance of the samples was then measured at 660 nm.

Carbohydrate estimation was carried out using a modified anthrone-sulfuric acid method. Initially, 300 mg of biomass was treated with 125 µL of concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and 3 mL of water, followed by autoclaving at 120 °C for 20 min. After the hydrolysis process, the solution was neutralized with calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. A 200 µL aliquot of the supernatant was mixed with 100 µL of 5 % phenol and 500 µL of 18 N sulfuric acid. The mixture was then incubated, and the absorbance was measured at 490 nm to quantify the carbohydrate content.

The quantification of photosynthetic pigments, such as chlorophyll a and b, was performed using Yang et al. [21]. 5 mL of the microalgae suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to concentrate the cells. The resulting pellet was then treated with 5 mL of 95 % methanol, followed by incubation in the dark at 4 °C for 24 h to allow pigment extraction. After a subsequent centrifugation step, the absorbance of the extracted supernatant was measured at 663 nm and 645 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 2600, Japan). The concentrations of chlorophyll a and b were determined using specific absorbance-based equations tailored to these wavelengths.

Dry biomass samples (50 mg) were subjected to sonication in a 3.9 mL methanol/chloroform mixture (2:1 v/v) at 60 °C. Phase separation was achieved by adding 2 mL of distilled water and 1.3 mL of chloroform to the mixture, followed by vortexing and centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The lower organic phase was collected, and a portion of it was transferred into a glass tube, where it was evaporated using a hot air oven at 60 °C. The lipid content was determined by calculating the difference in weight between the initial and final glass tubes, allowing for the estimation of lipid yield as a ratio of lipids to total algal biomass.

The energy content of microalgae biomass is derived from the total energy released through the oxidation of its key cellular components: carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins [23]. The biomass energy, denoted as EB (kJ), is calculated based on the dry weight of the biomass, Wb (g). The calorific values (standard values) for the complete oxidation of carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins are 16.7, 36.7, and 16.7 kJ/g, respectively, which contribute to the total energy yield from the biomass.
E
B
=
W
b
× [ 16.7 ×
C
C
+ 36.7 ×
C
l
+ 16.7 ×
C
p
]

For the analysis of superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, 50 mg of biomass was homogenized in 2 mL of 0.5 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) and subsequently centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The SOD activity in the supernatant was assessed by measuring the inhibition of nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT) reduction. The reaction mixture consisted of 1.5 mL of 1 M Na2CO3, 200 µL of 200 mM methionine, 100 µL of 2.25 mM NBT, 100 µL of 3 mM EDTA, 100 µL of 60 mM riboflavin, and 1.5 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.8). Absorbance was measured at 560 nm.

For catalase (CAT) activity analysis, 50 mg of biomass was homogenized in 2 mL of 0.5 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C. The resulting supernatant was used to measure CAT activity. The reaction mixture was prepared by combining 1.6 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.3), 100 µL of 3 mM EDTA, 200 µL of 0.3 % H2O2, and 100 µL of the enzyme-containing supernatant in a cuvette. The CAT activity was determined by monitoring the decomposition of H2O2, as indicated by a decrease in absorbance at 240 nm, using a blank of the same reaction mixture without H2O2 as a control.

All the experimental data were measured by IBM SPSS statistics software using ANOVA, t-test. The effect of graphene quantum dots in microalgal lutein, lipid, protein and carbohydrates were studied for statistically significant differences. The significance of results among values was set as P < 0.05.
Graphene QDs were taken in different concentrations to observe their positive effects on microalgal bioprocess for biomass and bioproduct enhancement in pre-optimized lutein bioprocess.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on biomass yield
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of GQDs (50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L) were introduced into a tubular photobioreactor, with a control group maintained without nanoparticle supplementation. The growth of microalgae showed enhancement at GQDs concentrations of 50 and 100 mg/L compared to the control. However, at higher concentrations of 150 and 200 mg/L, GQDs inhibited microalgae growth. Specifically, biomass production increased in the 50 and 100 mg/L groups, reaching 7.0 and 7.39 g/L, respectively, compared to the control's 6.8 g/L as depicted in Fig. 1 a. In contrast, the biomass decreased to 5.5 and 5.2 g/L in the 150 and 200 mg/L groups, respectively. Graphene, a carbon-based nanomaterial, can enhance microalgal growth and biomass production through several mechanisms [24]. It can convert and emit relatively more blue light, which is particularly beneficial for photosynthesis, as blue light is absorbed efficiently by chlorophyll and other photosynthetic pigments, thereby boosting the photosynthetic activity of microalgae [21]. Additionally, GQDs with high surface area may facilitate better light penetration and distribution within the culture, further enhancing photosynthetic efficiency. However, GQDs can lead to greater light absorption and increased stress conditions within the microalgae culture at higher concentrations. The excessive light absorption at higher concentrations can create an imbalance in the photosynthetic process, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidative stress, which ultimately inhibits growth and reduces biomass production. These findings suggest that while GQDs can be beneficial at lower concentrations, careful optimization is required to avoid adverse effects on microalgae growth at higher levels.

Nitrogen forms play a crucial role in microalgae growth and product accumulation, with specific nitrogen assimilation rates influencing the balance between biomass production and lutein synthesis, as observed in this study. Ammonium chloride is absorbed more rapidly than urea, as urea requires an additional conversion step to ammonium before uptake [25]. This delay in urea uptake promotes higher lutein accumulation. Urea undergoes a slower conversion to ammonium before it can be assimilated by microalgae, which generally causes a more gradual nitrogen release compared to direct ammonium sources like ammonium chloride. This controlled nitrogen availability may reduce the allocation of metabolic resources towards rapid biomass growth, allowing the microalgal cells to channel more energy and carbon into secondary metabolite production, including carotenoids like lutein. In the current study, biomass production decreased in control where ammonium chloride was replaced with urea, as the two provide nitrogen in different ionic forms [26],resulting in lower biomass yields as compared to previous experiment with ammonium form ranging 7.0–7.4 g/L. Control biomass was recorded at 5.52 g/L, while dosing with GQDs increased production: biomass reached 6.1 g/L at 50 mg/L GQDs and 6.6 g/L at 100 mg/L, as shown in Fig. 1b. However, higher GQD concentrations reduced biomass yield primarily due to two factors: light interference and oxidative stress. High concentrations of GQDs can absorb or scatter incoming light, limiting the light available for photosynthesis, which is essential for microalgal growth. Additionally, elevated levels of GQDs can generate oxidative stress within the cells, producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) that impair cellular functions and inhibit biomass accumulation [27,28]. Together, these factors hinder efficient photosynthesis and cellular health, leading to reduced biomass yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on lutein yield
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GQDs on lutein production was notably influenced by its concentration in the culture medium. At concentrations up to 100 mg/L, GQDs demonstrated a clear enhancement of lutein content, likely due to its ability to improve light absorption and nutrient exchange. The lutein content on the control was 12.04 mg/g. The quantum dot-incorporated microalgae sample showed an increased lutein content at particular concentrations, whereas in higher concentrations, the lutein content declined. The highest lutein content was observed at 100 mg/L, showing 14.69 mg/g of lutein content in dry biomass, followed by 50 mg/L, which was 13.1 mg/g. At higher concentrations 150 and 200 mg/L, the lutein content was low compared to the control, which was 9.05 mg/g and 8.4 mg/g, respectively (Fig. 2 a).

In the urea-based medium, the lutein content of the control set was 14.87 mg/g; it shows that the baseline lutein content increased in the control. The GQDs-incorporated microalgae also showed increased lutein content at 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L, which was 15.23 mg/g and 17.95 mg/g dry biomass. At higher concentrations of 150 and 200 mg/L, the lutein content was 14.71 mg/g and 12.05 mg/g respectively (Fig. 2b). This aligns with previous reports suggesting that graphene can facilitate more efficient photosynthetic processes, thereby stimulating pigment biosynthesis [29], including lutein. However, beyond this concentration, a decline in lutein production was observed. This reduction could be attributed to the excessive accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) or possible interference with nutrient availability and cell membrane integrity [30].

Lutein, a xanthophyll carotenoid, primarily serves as a light-harvesting pigment and a photoprotective agent in microalgae, accumulating during the growth stage when light and photosynthetic activity are high. As a crucial component of the photosynthetic apparatus, lutein helps absorb light energy and transfers it to chlorophyll for photosynthesis. This role in light harvesting is particularly important in environments with fluctuating light, as lutein broadens the absorption spectrum and improves overall light utilization efficiency [31,32].

In addition to its role in light harvesting, lutein plays a protective function by mitigating photooxidative stress. High light intensity or fluctuating light conditions can lead to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within photosynthetic cells, which can damage cellular structures. Lutein contributes to the quenching of singlet oxygen and other ROS, protecting the photosynthetic machinery from oxidative damage [33]. This photoprotective function is essential under highlight or nutrient-limited conditions, where ROS generation is more pronounced. The synthesis of lutein during the growth phase aligns with the microalgae’s need for both enhanced light capture and ROS mitigation, supporting both efficient energy capture and cellular protection. This dual role of lutein solar energy harvesting and ROS mitigation highlights why it accumulates during active growth, as both photosynthetic efficiency and cellular resilience are critical during this phase.

Furthermore, replacing equimolar ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) with urea in Beijerinck medium, in combination with GQDs, resulted in significantly higher lutein content than the GQDs + NH4Cl treatment. GQDs act as mild stressors, modulating intracellular ROS levels. ROS serve as signaling molecules that upregulate the secondary metabolic pathways, leading to the overexpression of key enzymes like phytoene synthase (PSY) and lycopene β-cyclase (LCYB), which are crucial for carotenoid biosynthesis, including lutein [34]. Urea, which undergoes enzymatic conversion to ammonia, may provide a more controlled and sustained nitrogen release, avoiding the potential ammonia effect associated with direct NH4Cl supplementation [25]. This gradual nitrogen supply, combined with GQDs enhanced nutrient and light capture capabilities, likely optimizes the metabolic environment for lutein biosynthesis [35]. Furthermore, urea might upregulate specific pathways related to lutein synthesis more effectively than ammonia or nitrate, leading to higher lutein yields [36]. Urease production is upregulated in urea-supplemented media, which splits urea into ammonia and CO2 to serve as nitrogen and carbon sources. In Chlorella sp., urease activity is induced with urea as a nitrogen source​ [37]. Therefore, the GQDs + urea system proves to be more effective in promoting lutein production compared to the GQDs + NH4Cl setup, highlighting the importance of nitrogen source selection in microalgal cultivation for pigment enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on protein yield
ce:section.ce:para: The observed increase in protein content in microalgae when GQDs are combined with urea, as compared to NH4Cl, can be attributed to differences in nitrogen assimilation pathways. In urea-based media, the protein content in the control was 33.2 %, which significantly rose to 44.2 % and 49.6 % with the addition of GQDs at 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L, respectively. However, at higher concentrations of 150 mg/L and 200 mg/L, the protein content dropped to 31.07 % and 28 %, respectively, falling below the control level. This decline at higher concentrations may be due to quantum dots interfering with protein synthesis pathways [38]. In NH4Cl-based media, the control protein content was 32 %, increasing to 41.4 % and 44 % with 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L of GQDs, respectively. However, at higher concentrations of 150 mg/L and 200 mg/L, the protein content significantly decreased to 23.9 % and 17 %, indicating increased stress on the cells (Fig. 3 a). The reason for these differences lies in the way nitrogen is metabolized. Urea is efficiently absorbed by microalgae and metabolized by enzyme urease, which releases ammonium and carbon dioxide key components that promote growth and enhance protein synthesis [37]. In contrast, NH4Cl-based media can disrupt the pH balance, leading to cellular stress and reduced protein production. Additionally, GQDs may facilitate nutrient uptake and improve nitrogen metabolism in urea-based media, further increasing protein content. At higher concentrations of GQDs (150 mg/L and 200 mg/L), oxidative stress and potential nanomaterial toxicity become more pronounced, particularly in NH4Cl-based media, which results in lower protein synthesis. In addition to inducing oxidative stress, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) may compromise cell membrane integrity, leading to inhibitory effects. In contrast, urea-based media mitigates these negative effects by supporting more efficient nitrogen metabolism, helping maintain or elevate protein levels [39]. This suggests that urea, in combination with GQDs, provides a more favorable environment for microalgae growth, optimizing nitrogen utilization and reducing the metabolic energy required for nitrogen assimilation compared to NH4Cl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on carbohydrate yield
ce:section.ce:para: The findings of this study demonstrate that the combination of GQDs and NH4Cl significantly enhances biomass and carbohydrate production in microalgae until 100 mg/L of GQDs. Beyond this concentration, at 150 and 200 mg/L, both biomass and carbohydrate content declined. This reduction suggests that while lower GQD concentrations facilitate improved growth and nutrient uptake, excessive GQDs levels may induce stress, negatively affecting growth and metabolic functions. In contrast, the combination of GQDs with urea also led to increased biomass at 50 and 100 mg/L, but these yields were a little higher compared to those observed in NH4Cl-based media. In NH4Cl-based media, the carbohydrate content in control was 19.3 %, whereas in 50 and 100 mg/L, the carbohydrate production notably increased to 21.5 % and 26.17 %. At 150 mg/L and 200 mg/L the carbohydrate production reduced to 12.8 % and 12.1 %. In a urea-based medium, the carbohydrate enhanced similar to that of NH4Cl. At control, the carbohydrate content was 21.93 %, 23.91 %, and 28 %, whereas at higher concentrations, the carbohydrate content decreased to 19.9 % and 13.6 %, respectively (Fig. 3b). Carbohydrate enhancement is due to enhanced light absorption and improved photosynthetic efficiency by adding quantum dots. The incorporation of quantum dots facilitates photosynthetic efficiency, which in turn leads to the production of carbohydrates as the process converts carbon dioxide and light energy into organic compounds [40]. Therefore, it is plausible to suggest that GQDs may enhance the carbon fixation pathway. As urea provided a distinct advantage in carbohydrate accumulation, surpassing the levels of NH4Cl media. This suggests that although biomass production was lower in the urea conditions, the slower nitrogen assimilation allows for greater allocation of resources towards carbohydrate synthesis, highlighting the trade-offs between growth and nutrient composition in microalgae cultivation [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on chlorophyll yield
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GQDs on chlorophyll content in microalgae was evaluated under different nitrogen supplementation conditions. It was observed that GQDs, when used alone at concentrations of 50 and 100 mg/L, enhanced chlorophyll content significantly than the control. This suggests that GQD facilitates improved photosynthetic efficiency, possibly by enhancing light absorption and electron transfer directly benefiting chlorophyll production [41]. In a urea-based medium, the chlorophyll content rose gradually and exhibited up to 37.65 µg/mL and 45.3 µg/mL in both chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b at 100 mg/L. In higher concentrations of 150 and 200 mg/L, the chlorophyll a and b were reduced than the control. In NH4Cl-based media, the chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b content increased profoundly till 100 mg/L and reduced at subsequent GODs concentrations rise (Fig. 4 a–d). However, the response of chlorophyll content varied when different nitrogen sources were combined with GQDs. Specifically, combining GQDs with NH4Cl resulted in a lesser increase in chlorophyll content than GQDs combined with urea. The urea-based supplementation, in conjunction with GQDs, showed a more pronounced enhancement in chlorophyll content [42]. This could be attributed to the more efficient nitrogen assimilation mechanism of urea in the presence of GQDs, which may promote better nutrient availability and photosynthetic activity. Photosynthesis is very essential for the microalgae growth and its resilience to external environmental changes. It maintains a delicate balance between the microalgae's ability to resist photodamage and its capacity for recovery [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on lipid yield
ce:section.ce:para: The total lipid content of Chlorella variabilis showed a notable increase up to a certain concentration of graphene nanoparticles, with lipid accumulation peaking at 150 mg/L. At this concentration, the lipid content reached its highest value compared to the control. However, at 200 mg/L, the lipid content, along with biomass, showed a marked reduction, likely due to increased oxidative stress caused by excessive nanoparticle exposure. The addition of nanoparticles may trigger a stress response in microalgae, which often leads to the accumulation of lipids as an energy storage material. Lipid biosynthesis is frequently upregulated under stress conditions, as cells shift towards storing excess carbon in the form of lipids to cope with adverse environmental stressors [40,44]. In NH4Cl-based medium, the initial lipid content in the control was 20.4 %, and the incorporation of nanoparticles led to a significant increase, reaching 26.5 % at 150 mg/L (Fig. 3c). This rise suggests that NH4Cl, combined with GQDs, can enhance lipid synthesis, possibly by promoting carbon fixation and redirecting metabolic pathways toward lipid production under mild stress. In a urea-based medium, where urea serves as a modified nitrogen source, the lipid content of the control was 21 %. Upon exposure to graphene nanoparticles, the lipid content increased gradually, reaching 22.2 %, 24.6 %, and 26.5 % at 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, and 150 mg/L concentrations, respectively. This modest rise in lipid content, compared to the NH4Cl medium, may be attributed to differences in nitrogen assimilation, where urea metabolism does not induce as strong a stress response as NH4Cl, resulting in a slower but steady accumulation of lipids. However, at the highest concentration of 200 mg/L, the lipid content in both media dropped significantly. In NH4Cl-based medium, the lipid content fell from 26.5 % at 150 mg/L to 19–20 % at 200 mg/L, indicating that the excessive concentration of nanoparticles overwhelmed the cellular stress tolerance, reducing their ability to sustain with high lipid synthesis. The decline in biomass at 200 mg/L further supports the hypothesis that oxidative stress at high GQDs concentrations impairs overall metabolic function, leading to reduced cell count and lipid accumulation. The heatmap illustrates the relative abundance of three intracellular compounds under two different conditions in the presence of graphene quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b. Protein levels exhibit higher intensity compared to lipids and carbohydrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots on overall bioenergy potential
ce:section.ce:para: The biorefinery potential of the microalgae depends upon the amount of biomass produced by microalgae as it serves as the foundation for generating bioenergy and other value-added products [45]. At 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L, bioenergy potential was notably enhanced, which can be attributed to the ability of quantum dots to convert light energy more efficiently thereby boosting the photosynthetic activity and biomass accumulation. However, at higher concentrations (150 mg/L and 200 mg/L), bioenergy potential reduces due to the excessive light engagement in photooxidation [27], which results in the reduction of microalgal growth. The increased light stress negatively impacted cell proliferation leading to reduced biomass and diminished bioenergy output. Furthermore, the NH4Cl-based medium promoted greater biomass production and increased bioenergy output. In contrast, the urea-based medium with and without quantum dots boosted specific bioenergy components but led to low bioenergy production due to the lower biomass yield. At 100 mg/L, the overall bioenergy enhanced notably in both urea and NH4Cl-based, exhibiting 895 and 985 kJ energy. At higher concentrations (200 mg/L), the bioenergy resulted in decreased production, exhibiting 356 and 254 kJ (Fig. 6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of graphene quantum dots in oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: In metabolic processes such as photosynthesis and respiration, plant cells generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), including superoxide radicals (O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (HO•), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). These ROS are by-products of photosynthetic electron transport and can also originate from mitochondria, peroxisomes, and plasma membranes via NADPH oxidases [46,47]. Moreover, ROS can accumulate during cellular activities like division, differentiation, growth, and apoptosis. To mitigate ROS, cells have evolved both enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant defense systems, including catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), ascorbate peroxidase (APX), ascorbic acid (AsA), glutathione, tocopherols, and carotenoids such as lutein and astaxanthin. Both plants and microalgae activate multiple defense mechanisms to neutralize diverse ROS species effectively.

Under adverse conditions, such as intense light exposure, drought, salinity, and nutrient deficiencies, ROS production in plant cells increases. Nanomaterials, such as GQDs, can also induce ROS generation, triggering oxidative stress mechanisms within cells [27]. To assess the oxidative stress caused by graphene nanoparticles (NPs), intracellular levels of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and SOD were measured during the growth of Chlorella variabilis. H2O2, a type of superoxide radical, can accumulate under external stress and potentially harm the organism. However, the organism manages oxidative damage by upregulating antioxidant enzymes, reducing excess H2O2 to manageable levels.

The activities of SOD and CAT increased significantly at higher GQDs concentrations (150 and 200 mg/L), while only slight increases were observed at lower concentrations (50 and 100 mg/L). At these lower concentrations, GQDs exposure caused minimal oxidative stress, reflected by modest increases in SOD and CAT activity. For instance, at 50 mg/L, SOD and CAT activities increased by 9 % and 16 %, respectively, in urea-based media, while CAT activity rose by 8 % and 13 % in NH4Cl-based media. At 100 mg/L, SOD activity increased further to 15 % and 18 %, with CAT activity rising to 11 % and 15 %. However, at higher concentrations (150 and 200 mg/L), the oxidative stress response was much more pronounced. At 150 mg/L, SOD activity rose to 41.4 % and 45 %, with CAT activity reaching 38 % and 35 %. At 200 mg/L, SOD and CAT activities peaked, with SOD reaching 60 % and CAT at 50 % (Fig. 7 a and b). These increases reflect the greater oxidative stress induced by higher GQDs concentrations, prompting the upregulation of these antioxidant enzymes to neutralize excess ROS and prevent cellular damage. SOD converts superoxide radicals into hydrogen peroxide, which is subsequently broken down by CAT into water and oxygen. This highlights the critical role of these enzymes in managing cellular oxidative stress [28]. Since the product of the SOD reaction (H2O2) serves as the substrate for the CAT reaction, the activities of these enzymes are closely correlated. Thus, their simultaneous upregulation at higher GQDs concentrations reflects the increased oxidative stress caused by the nanomaterials, with both enzymes working in context to detoxify ROS.

These results indicate that lutein is not the primary compound synthesized by microalgae to suppress ROS; instead, enzymatic pathways appear to be the dominant mechanism for ROS mitigation. Consequently, lutein production does not show a direct correlation with increased ROS levels. This suggests that lutein synthesis is primarily driven by other factors, with optimal growth conditions and favorable nitrogen assimilation playing key roles in its production.

In this study, the direct exposure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) demonstrated concentration-dependent effects on microalgal growth and biomass yield. At elevated GQD concentrations, microalgal growth was significantly inhibited, resulting in reduced biomass production. This suppression was primarily attributed to enhanced oxidative stress, which adversely affected the synthesis of intracellular compounds and disrupted normal cellular processes. Conversely, at lower GQD concentrations, microalgae exhibited a notable enhancement in biomass production. Specifically, at a concentration of 100 mg/L, GQDs showed the most pronounced positive effects, leading to enhanced biomass, lutein, protein, and carbohydrate synthesis. Lutein production increased by 30.5 %, yielding 118.2 mg/L, indicating improved carotenoid synthesis and cellular growth. This optimal concentration contributed significantly to the bioenergy potential of microalgae, highlighting its utility in promoting microalgal growth and intracellular compound accumulation under controlled conditions.

Anil Kumar Patel: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yamini Sumathi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Reeta Rani Singhania: Validation, Supervision. Chiu-Wen Chen: Resources. Philippe Michaud: Validation, Supervision. Cheng-Di Dong: Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: RRS and AKP acknowledge the NSTC, Taiwan, for the grant (Ref. No. NSTC 112-2222-E-992-006-MY2 and 113-2221-E-992-006).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-dose responses of Scenedesmus capricornus microalgae exposed to humic acid
Sci. Total Environ.
